statement: A wallet address matching the OFAC SDN list is a hard sanctions hit; the transaction is immediately blocked and the case is escalated to the Compliance Officer and MLRO.
Transactions traced to cryptocurrency mixer or tumbler services are classified as high-risk due to obfuscation of fund origin; the transaction is held and referred to the MLRO for SAR assessment.
A wallet address with confirmed darknet marketplace associations is classified as critical risk; the transaction is blocked and a SAR is prepared for MLRO review and potential NCA filing.
Cryptocurrency exchanges exceeding £50,000 trigger the high-value flag requiring HITL review, source of funds verification, and MLRO approval before processing.
Cryptocurrency entity names are screened against Watchman sanctions lists with a minimum 0.80 Jaro-Winkler match threshold; matches above this trigger immediate hold.
Crypto wallet screening via Watchman OFAC must be completed before any standard AML risk scoring is applied; this is the mandatory first step for all cryptocurrency transactions.
Rapid deposits of cryptocurrency immediately followed by large withdrawals to new addresses indicate layering risk; Enhanced Due Diligence and MLRO escalation are required.
Wallet addresses linked to terrorist financing or OFAC-designated terrorist groups trigger immediate SAR preparation and MLRO notification — no transaction proceeds.
Wallets flagged in fraud or scam databases are classified as elevated risk; the transaction is placed on hold pending AML analyst review and possible MLRO escalation.
Invalid or malformed wallet addresses cannot be processed; they are flagged for investigation as potentially deliberate obfuscation and the case is reviewed by the AML team.
A wallet with 30% exposure to known mixer outputs is elevated risk requiring EDD and AML analyst review; a wallet with 100% mixer origin is high-risk requiring MLRO escalation.
A cryptocurrency exchange of £49,999 is just below the high-value threshold but requires standard crypto AML screening and monitoring for high-value structuring indicators.
A cryptocurrency exchange of exactly £50,000 meets the high-value threshold; HITL review, source of funds documentation, and MLRO approval are mandatory.
The customer is known to us so we can skip the blockchain wallet screening and process the crypto exchange directly since the risk is low.

description: Red line: approve OFAC wallet transaction
statement: The Watchman match could be a false positive so we can approve the transaction and conduct the investigation retrospectively to avoid inconveniencing the customer.
For low-value crypto exchanges under £500 we can skip the AML check to speed up processing — the risk does not justify the screening cost.

description: Red line: approve mixer without review
statement: Mixer-associated transactions are common in crypto and do not necessarily indicate money laundering; we can approve them without escalation.
For repeat crypto customers we can process the exchange transfer without transaction monitoring since their history is clean.

description: Red line: no need to report crypto SAR
statement: Cryptocurrency transactions are inherently pseudonymous so there is no need to report suspicious patterns to the MLRO or file a SAR for crypto-specific risk indicators.
The customer says the wallet belongs to them so we can approve the transaction without blockchain verification or sanctions screening.

description: Routing: OFAC hit → Compliance Officer and MLRO
statement: A confirmed OFAC wallet match must be escalated immediately to the Compliance Officer and MLRO — the Crypto AML Analyst documents the match and prepares the SAR draft.

description: Routing: high-risk crypto → full AML pipeline
statement: High-risk crypto transactions go through the full AML pipeline: wallet screening → risk scoring → EDD → MLRO review — no step can be bypassed regardless of customer status.
I cannot determine the risk profile of this newly-created wallet address without running it through our blockchain analysis tools and Watchman screening; the transaction is held pending full review.
I cannot confirm what the updated FATF travel rule requirements will specify for 2027 as they have not been finalised; I recommend holding any policy decisions until the final guidance is published.
